---
title: "FAQ"
---

```{=html}
<div>




<div id="header">



<h1 class="title toc-ignore">Frequently Asked Questions</h1>
<h4 class="date">Updated on 2022-07-14</h4>

</div>

<div id="TOC">
<ul>
<li><a href="#general-questions">General Questions</a>
<ul>
<li><a href="#what-is-wordbank">What is Wordbank?</a></li>
<li><a href="#what-can-i-do-with-the-site">What can I do with the
site?</a></li>
<li><a href="#how-many-cdis-are-in-the-database">How many CDIs are in
the database?</a></li>
<li><a href="#i-am-interested-in-using-the-cdis-for-my-own-projects-how-do-i-get-the-forms-themselves">I
am interested in using the CDIs for my own projects, how do I get the
forms themselves?</a></li>
</ul></li>
<li><a href="#using-wordbank">Using Wordbank</a>
<ul>
<li><a href="#do-i-have-to-know-r-to-use-wordbank">Do I have to know R
to use Wordbank?</a></li>
<li><a href="#can-i-work-with-the-data-on-my-own-rather-than-through-the-website">Can
I work with the data on my own, rather than through the
website?</a></li>
<li><a href="#can-i-use-wordbank-in-my-undergraduate-or-graduate-classes">Can I
use Wordbank in my undergraduate or graduate classes?</a></li>
<li><a href="#i-need-to-find-words-to-use-as-stimuli-for-a-study-that-im-running.-can-wordbank-help">I
need to find words to use as stimuli for a study that I’m running. Can
Wordbank help?</a></li>
</ul></li>
<li><a href="#contributing-to-wordbank">Contributing to Wordbank</a>
<ul>
<li><a href="#who-can-contribute-to-the-database">Who can contribute to
the database?</a></li>
<li><a href="#why-should-i-contribute-to-wordbank">Why should I
contribute to Wordbank?</a></li>
<li><a href="#do-my-data-need-to-be-published-before-i-can-contribute">Do my
data need to be published before I can contribute?</a></li>
<li><a href="#i-want-to-contribute-now-what-do-i-do">I want to
contribute, now what do I do?</a></li>
<li><a href="#does-sharing-my-data-with-wordbank-violate-human-subjects-protections">Does
sharing my data with Wordbank violate Human Subjects
protections?</a></li>
</ul></li>
<li><a href="#about-wordbank">About Wordbank</a>
<ul>
<li><a href="#ive-used-the-database-for-an-analysis-that-im-writing-up.-how-do-i-cite-it">I’ve
used the database for an analysis that I’m writing up. How do I cite
it?</a></li>
<li><a href="#what-is-the-license-for-derivative-works-from-wordbank-e.g.-graphs-generated-by-the-site">What
is the license for derivative works from Wordbank (e.g., graphs
generated by the site)?</a></li>
<li><a href="#how-did-you-build-wordbank">How did you build
Wordbank?</a></li>
<li><a href="#who-funds-wordbank">Who funds Wordbank?</a></li>
</ul></li>
</ul>
</div>

<p></p>
<div id="general-questions" class="section level2">
<h2>General Questions</h2>
<div id="what-is-wordbank" class="section level3">
<h3>What is Wordbank?</h3>
<p>Wordbank is a site for archiving, sharing, and exploring anonymized
MacArthur-Bates Communicative Development Inventory (CDIs) data from the
original English form and from CDI adaptations in many languages (such
as Croatian, Danish, English, German, Italian, Norwegian, Russian,
Spanish, Swedish, and Turkish). Wordbank compiles responses from norming
studies but also includes data that individual researchers have
contributed from various research projects, large and small. As a
successor to CLEX, Wordbank allows users to generate norms for
populations and for individual items interactively, and can be used as
both a teaching and a research tool. For background and some working
examples, see <a href="http://langcog.stanford.edu/papers_new/frank-2016-jcl.pdf">Frank
et al. (2017)</a>.</p>
<p>If you want more information about Wordbank and the CDI, take a look
at our book, <a href="https://langcog.github.io/wordbank-book/">Variability and
Consistency in Early Language Learning: The Wordbank Project</a>.</p>
</div>
<div id="what-can-i-do-with-the-site" class="section level3">
<h3>What can I do with the site?</h3>
<p>Wordbank is designed to be flexible and interactive, allowing users
to generate analyses for groups of children (e.g., how many words do
English-speaking girls produce between 16 and 24 months?) or for
individual items (e.g., how many children are reported to produce the
word “dog” at 15 months?). Since Wordbank contains data from many
different languages, it allows you to explore both child-level and
item-level data within and across languages. On the Analyses page,
select the <em>Vocabulary Norms</em> tab to track normative growth in
vocabulary in a given language across all children in the database, or
split by gender, maternal education or birth order. Select the <em>Item
Trajectories</em> tab to plot the trajectory of acquisition for one or
more individual items/words, or the <em>Cross-Linguistic
Trajectories</em> tab to compare item frequencies across languages. Each
analysis page has a <em>Download CSV</em> button for the current view, and
the <em>Data</em> page lets you filter and export any table in the database
(including the full child-by-word data) directly from Redivis.</p>
</div>
<div id="how-many-cdis-are-in-the-database" class="section level3">
<h3>How many CDIs are in the database?</h3>
<p>The number of CDIs stored in Wordbank is continually growing, and we
are always interested in adding more datasets to our site! To see which
researchers have already contributed, click here: <a href="contributors.html">the Contributors page</a>. If you are
interested in contributing your own data, please contact us via email at
<a href="mailto:wordbank-contact@stanford.edu" class="email">wordbank-contact@stanford.edu</a>.</p>
</div>
<div id="i-am-interested-in-using-the-cdis-for-my-own-projects-how-do-i-get-the-forms-themselves" class="section level3">
<h3>I am interested in using the CDIs for my own projects, how do I get
the forms themselves?</h3>
<p>To learn more about the CDI instruments themselves, including
information about the many different languages for which CDI instruments
are available, go to the <a href="http://mb-cdi.stanford.edu">CDI
website</a>. The MacArthur-Bates CDIs for English and Mexican Spanish
are available for purchase from Brookes Publishing Company: <a href="http://www.brookespublishing.com" class="uri">http://www.brookespublishing.com</a>. If you are interested
in a CDI adaptation in other languages, consult the <a href="http://mb-cdi.stanford.edu/adaptations">Adaptations page on the
CDI website</a> and contact the developer of that instrument
directly.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="using-wordbank" class="section level2">
<h2>Using Wordbank</h2>
<div id="do-i-have-to-know-r-to-use-wordbank" class="section level3">
<h3>Do I have to know R to use Wordbank?</h3>
<p>No! All of the code for running analyses with Wordbank data are
available using the <code>wordbankr</code> package, but we have set up
many Wordbank analyses and visualizations that you can do interactively
directly through the website. No knowledge of R is necessary.</p>
</div>
<div id="can-i-work-with-the-data-on-my-own-rather-than-through-the-website" class="section level3">
<h3>Can I work with the data on my own, rather than through the
website?</h3>
<p>No problem. All Wordbank tables are hosted on
<a href="https://redivis.com/datapages/datasets/wordbank">Redivis</a>, where
you can filter and export them in csv or parquet format (the Data page
embeds the most-used tables). The dataset is versioned, so you can cite and
retrieve the exact release you analyzed. You can also access the data
directly in R by using the <a href="https://github.com/langcog/wordbankr"><code>wordbankr</code>
package</a>.</p>
</div>
<div id="can-i-use-wordbank-in-my-undergraduate-or-graduate-classes" class="section level3">
<h3>Can I use Wordbank in my undergraduate or graduate classes?</h3>
<p>Absolutely! Wordbank is designed to be user-friendly and to enable
students from all backgrounds to explore data on early language
development. For example, students can use the interactive tools to
identify those words that are earliest or latest learned in English or
other languages, compare the trajectory of acquisition for particular
words across languages, or compare general trends in vocabulary growth
as a function of gender or maternal education. We would love to hear
about how you are using Wordbank in your classes, including feedback for
how we could make Wordbank a better tool for this use!</p>
</div>
<div id="i-need-to-find-words-to-use-as-stimuli-for-a-study-that-im-running.-can-wordbank-help" class="section level3">
<h3>I need to find words to use as stimuli for a study that I’m running.
Can Wordbank help?</h3>
<p>Yes! To visually explore trajectories of acquisition using our
interactive tools, try out the plots in the <em>Item Trajectories</em>
section or compare acquisition for a single word across many different
languages in our <em>Cross-Linguistic Trajectories</em> section.
However, a more comprehensive way to obtain item frequencies is via the
<em>By-Word Summary Data</em> in the <em>Data Export Tools</em> section.
Click on the Language, Form, and Measure that you are interested in
(e.g., English, Produces, Words &amp; Sentences), and then use the
selector to identify the Age (Months) (e.g., 24-30 months). In this
example, the resulting table shows the proportion of children who are
reported to produce each item at each age. You can browse the entire
table or sort the output by a selected column (e.g., sort words
alphabetically, from highest-to-lowest frequency, or by CDI category).
You can download the complete table, if you wish, or use the Search box
in the upper right hand corner to select particular items. Search for a
category name (e.g., animals) if you want to see all of the words in a
particular category.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="contributing-to-wordbank" class="section level2">
<h2>Contributing to Wordbank</h2>
<div id="who-can-contribute-to-the-database" class="section level3">
<h3>Who can contribute to the database?</h3>
<p>Any researcher can contribute to Wordbank. Many of the datasets
contributed so far represent norming data, but Wordbank is designed to
incorporate CDI administrations that were collected for many different
purposes and in research projects of many different sizes. We welcome
your data regardless of whether you have hundreds (or even thousands) of
forms, or if you only have a few dozen!</p>
</div>
<div id="why-should-i-contribute-to-wordbank" class="section level3">
<h3>Why should I contribute to Wordbank?</h3>
<p>By contributing to Wordbank, you will become part of a consortium of
researchers who believe that, by combining our data together, we
increase our power to understand children’s early language development.
Following in the spirit of <a href="http://childes.psy.cmu.edu/">CHILDES</a>, <a href="https://nyu.databrary.org/">Databrary</a>, <a href="http://www.cdi-clex.org/">CLEX</a>, and others, we hope that being
a part of Wordbank will raise the visibility of your work, while
facilitating crosslinguistic analyses of CDI data by researchers around
the world!</p>
</div>
<div id="do-my-data-need-to-be-published-before-i-can-contribute" class="section level3">
<h3>Do my data need to be published before I can contribute?</h3>
<p>Not necessarily. You are welcome to submit your data at any time in
the publication process (or if you don’t plan on publishing at all). You
could even include a note about contributing to Wordbank in your
manuscript submission. If you have published your data at time of
submission, we will cite the corresponding publication along with your
name on the contributors’ page (and we can always update this if you
publish a manuscript later).</p>
</div>
<div id="i-want-to-contribute-now-what-do-i-do" class="section level3">
<h3>I want to contribute, now what do I do?</h3>
<p>If you have electronic, item-level data in any format, you are
welcome to forward on what you have and we will work to convert it to
Wordbank format. It is important that each child record has a unique
identifier and that children with multiple administrations are provided
with the same ID for each administration. Additional demographic
information is also very helpful, especially child’s gender, mother’s
year of education, birth weight, gestational age, and birth order.
(Please provide a key to your variable values!). If your data include
children from special populations, such as children with ASD or children
born preterm, please indicate that as well. If you are contributing data
for a language that is not currently in Wordbank, please provide a copy
of the CDI form itself, a translation of the items, and any related
publications or working papers. Once the data are imported, we will
acknowledge your work on the contributors’ page and post any citations
related to the data (see <a href="contributors.html">the Contributors page</a>). To assure
that your data were imported correctly, will send you a link to your
particular dataset so that you can verify our work.</p>
<p>If you have only hard copy data, please contact us. It may be
possible for us to help in digitizing the data.</p>
</div>
<div id="does-sharing-my-data-with-wordbank-violate-human-subjects-protections" class="section level3">
<h3>Does sharing my data with Wordbank violate Human Subjects
protections?</h3>
<p>No! All of the data in Wordbank are completely anonymized and
therefore, do not constitute human subjects research. Potentially
identifying information such as date of birth is used to compute child’s
age of test, but is never stored in the database. We assume that your
data were collected following your own institution’s IRB standards and
should be completely anonymized before contributing to Wordbank.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="about-wordbank" class="section level2">
<h2>About Wordbank</h2>
<div id="ive-used-the-database-for-an-analysis-that-im-writing-up.-how-do-i-cite-it" class="section level3">
<h3>I’ve used the database for an analysis that I’m writing up. How do I
cite it?</h3>
<p>Please cite publications for the specific datasets you use, as well
as our 2017 Journal of Child Language paper on the dataset. <a href="/contributors">You can see our our citation policy for more
information.</a></p>
</div>
<div id="what-is-the-license-for-derivative-works-from-wordbank-e.g.-graphs-generated-by-the-site" class="section level3">
<h3>What is the license for derivative works from Wordbank (e.g., graphs
generated by the site)?</h3>
<p>Unless otherwise stated, Wordbank and its datasets are licensed under
a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, <a href="https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/">CC BY 4.0</a>. If
you use Wordbank in a derivative work, you must give credit, but
otherwise you are free to do anything else you want with most products
from the site.<br />
Some datasets listed under the <a href="http://wordbank.stanford.edu/contributors">Contributors page</a>
may be marked with a <img alt="CC BY NC License 4.0" src="images/ccbync.large.png" style='width: 2%;'> logo. This means these
particular datasets are licensed under a Creative Commons
Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License, <a href="https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/">CC BY-NC 4.0</a>.
These datasets may not be used for commercial purposes but otherwise are
free to use and adapt as long as credit is given.</p>
</div>
<div id="how-did-you-build-wordbank" class="section level3">
<h3>How did you build Wordbank?</h3>
<p>Wordbank is a static website: the data live in a versioned
<a href="https://redivis.com/datapages/datasets/wordbank">Redivis dataset</a>,
pages are built with <a href="https://quarto.org">Quarto</a>, and all
interactive visualization runs in your browser using
<a href="https://observablehq.com/plot/">Observable Plot</a> and
<a href="https://duckdb.org/docs/api/wasm/overview.html">DuckDB</a>.
All of the source code is open and available at
<a href="https://github.com/langcog/wordbank-datapage">github</a>. If you see
something you want to fix or contribute to, send us a pull request or
let us know!</p>
</div>
<div id="who-funds-wordbank" class="section level3">
<h3>Who funds Wordbank?</h3>
<p>The development of Wordbank was funded by a grant from the National
Science Foundation (# 1451577) as well as generous support from the
MB-CDI Advisory Board.</p>
</div>
</div>




</div>



<!-- tabsets -->



<!-- code folding -->


<!-- dynamically load mathjax for compatibility with self-contained -->
```
